texts.text: 1. Introduction

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Due to an increased demand for helium in combination with dwindling resources, a shortage of the supply of the gas has been experienced in  recent  years  [1,2].  Helium  is  increasingly  used  in  scientific  and medical instruments as well as for aerospace, electronics and cryogenic technologies. Helium is typically recovered from natural gas reserves in the United States where helium concentration can reach up to 4 mol% but is increasingly extracted from less helium-rich gas reserves [3]. The development and implementation of new, more efficient technologies for production of helium is therefore needed [4].

texts.label: text
texts.orig: In addition to methane and other hydrocarbons, natural gas consists of nitrogen, water vapour, carbon dioxide and helium. The concentration of helium varies from trace amounts up to 4% depending on the location. For instance, the natural gas in Alaska has a composition of about 3% helium, 7% nitrogen and 90% methane as main components [3].  Moreover,  a  helium  concentration  of  above  0.3%  is  considered economic for extraction [4]. In a natural gas processing plant for helium recovery,  CO2,  H2O and  other  impurities  are  removed  before  CH4  is separated  from  the  mixture  by  liquefaction  using  a  cryogenic  heat exchanger. After this step, the helium concentration is typically 1 -3%. Following this, a very expensive and energy intensive process using a combination of cryogenic distillation and pressure swing adsorption is used to produce helium of high purity [5]. texts.label: text
texts.orig: The development of more efficient technology for the production of helium from natural gas is pressing as the current resources are dwindling. In the present work, ultra-thin MFI membranes were evaluated for the separation of an equimolar CH4/N2/He mixture in a wide temperature range 120 -293K. The membrane was highly selective towards CH4  and N2  at all the investigated conditions, which resulted in a helium rich retentate. The observed selectivity should be a result of selective adsorption of CH4  and N2. A maximum (CH4 + N2)/He separation factor of 152 was observed at 153 K and a feed pressure of 3 bar and a permeate pressure of 0.2 bar. At these conditions, separation factors were 265 and 38 for CH4/He and N2/He, respectively, and the CH4 and N2 fluxes were 1.12 and 0.16 mol/(m 2 ⋅ s),  respectively. To the best of our knowledge, these are the best results reported in the open literature for the recovery of helium from natural gas using membrane technology. The high selectivity and flux indicated that the ultra-thin MFI membranes are a promising candidate for efficient helium production from natural gas.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The possibility of using membrane technology for separation of helium from He/N2  or He/CH4  mixtures has been investigated using a variety of organic and inorganic membranes [10 -15]. High selectivities for He/N2  and He/CH4  of 34 and 31, respectively, with a helium permeance of 0.4 × 10 7 mol/(m 2 ⋅ s ⋅ Pa) at room temperature have been reported  for  cellulose  acetate  membranes  [12].  For  inorganic  membranes, a very high He/N2 ideal selectivity of 560 with a single gas He permeance of 22 × 10 7 mol/(m 2 ⋅ s ⋅ Pa) has been reported for microporous silica membranes [13]. For He/CH4  separation, a selectivity of 147 with a He permeance of 9.8 × 10 7 mol/(m 2 ⋅ s ⋅ Pa)  [14],  and  a selectivity of 500 with a He permeance of 0.5 × 10 7 mol/(m 2 ⋅ s ⋅ Pa) [15] have also been reported for silica membranes. Although these ideal selectivities (single gas permeance ratios) are impressive, mixture separation  selectivity  is  not  reported  in  most  of  these  studies  and  the permeance is low. However, selectivities of above 20 are adequate for commercial use [10] and therefore high permeance is highly demanded

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Due to the well-defined pores and inorganic nature, ceramic zeolite membranes may potentially display both high permeability and selectivity and higher chemical and thermal stability than polymeric membranes and are therefore very promising candidates for gas separations. In addition, to be competitive, zeolite membranes must probably show much higher permeance compared to polymeric membranes due to the higher costs of ceramic materials. Our research group has developed a method for synthesis of ultra-thin (~500 nm) MFI zeolite membranes which  have  exhibited  high  selectivities  coupled  with  a  significantly higher permeance than other organic, inorganic or zeolite membranes (10 -100 times higher) for separation of various gas and liquid mixtures [16 -19].  Recently,  these  membranes  have  been  evaluated for  N2/He separation and a high separation selectivity of 76 coupled with a high N2 permeance of 39 × 10 7 mol/(m 2 ⋅ s ⋅ Pa) at a temperature of 124 K was observed [6].

texts.orig: 2.1. Membranes preparation and characterisation
texts.text: 2.1. Membranes preparation and characterisation

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The  MFI  membranes  used in  the  present  work  were  provided  by ZeoMem Sweden AB. Porous α -alumina discs with a diameter of 25 mm and a thickness of 3 mm were used as supports. The membranes were calcined at 500 ◦ C for 6 h at a heating rate of 0.2 ◦ C/min and a cooling rate of 0.3 ◦ C/min before use.
texts.text: The  MFI  membranes  used in  the  present  work  were  provided  by ZeoMem Sweden AB. Porous α -alumina discs with a diameter of 25 mm and a thickness of 3 mm were used as supports. The membranes were calcined at 500 ◦ C for 6 h at a heating rate of 0.2 ◦ C/min and a cooling rate of 0.3 ◦ C/min before use.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The membrane was mounted in a Wicke-Kallenbach stainless steel cell sealed with graphite gaskets. The defect distribution of the membrane was measured using n -hexane/helium adsorption-branch permporometry [21,22]. In short, the membrane was dried at 573 K for 6 h in a helium flow prior to the measurement. The pressure difference across the membrane was 1 bar with an atmospheric permeate pressure. The permeance  of  helium  through  the  membrane  was  determined  as  a function of the relative pressure of n -hexane in the feed. When n -hexane was added to the feed, the helium permeance decreased gradually. The defect area distribution was calculated from the measured helium molar flow and helium molar flux through the defects estimated from Fick ' s law. The width of defects smaller and larger than 2 nm was estimated by the  Horvath-  Kawazoe  (H -K)  equation  and  the  Kelvin  equation, respectively. Defect distribution was evaluated in terms of relative areas of defects (area of defects divided by the total membrane area). The morphology of the membranes was investigated using a FEI Magellan 400 field emission XHR-scanning electron microscope (SEM).

texts.orig: 2.2. Gas separation
texts.text: 2.2. Gas separation

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The temperature in the cell was measured by a type K thermocouple. The membrane cell was mounted in a cryostat as described in previous work [6]. Before separation experiments, the membrane was dried at 573 K for 8 h under a flow of helium with a heating rate of 1 ◦ C/min followed by natural cooling in the vacuum chamber. The desired feed flow rate was achieved by using digital mass flow controllers (Bronkhorst,  HI-TEC).  The  permeate  pressure  was  controlled  by  a  vacuum pump (Pfeiffer Vacuum, MVP 040). The pressure on either side of the membrane was measured by mechanical pressure gauges. A bubble flow meter was used to measure the permeate flow rate, and the composition of feed and permeate streams was analysed using a Micro GC (490 Micro GC, Agilent) connected online. An equimolar CH4/N2/He mixture prepared  by  mixing  pure  nitrogen  (99.999%),  methane  (99.995%)  and helium (99.999%) was purchased from the company AGA gas and used for separation experiments.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The  separation  experiments  were  carried  out  in  the  temperature range 120 -293K. Feed pressures of 3 and 6 bar were investigated while the  permeate  pressures  were  maintained  at  0.2  or  0.3  bar.  The  feed flowrate was 10 Nl/min to eliminate effects of concentration polarisation.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: nHexane/helium  permporometry  was  employed  to  evaluate  the defect distribution of the membranes [21]. We have published several papers focussing on the fabrication of MFI membranes and evaluation of the  membrane quality by permporometry earlier [20 -22,27,28]. The present worked is instead focused on a detailed evaluation of the separation performance of one single MFI membrane in a new application. The membrane used in the present work showed an initial helium permeance as high as 78 × 10 7 mol/(m 2 ⋅ s ⋅ Pa) at p/p0 = 0, see Table 1. This is  comparable  with  previously  reported  helium  permeances  of  81 × 10 7 mol/(m 2 ⋅ s ⋅ Pa) [20] for MFI membranes with a thickness of around 500  nm.  The  helium  permeance  reduces  drastically  as n -hexane  is introduced  due  to  blocking  of  helium  transport  through  the  zeolite pores. This drastic change is characteristic of a high quality membrane with few defects [21]. The helium permeance decreased by 99.7 % at an n -hexane relative pressure of 0.023, corresponding to a defect width of 0.94  nm. As  the  relative  pressure  was  further  increased  to  0.80,  the helium permeance reduced to 0.05 × 10 7 mol/(m 2 ⋅ s ⋅ Pa), i.e. decreased by 99.9 %. This relative pressure corresponds to a defect width of 17 nm. Table 1 also shows that the relative area (defect area/membrane area) of these defects with a size larger than 17 nm is estimated to be as low as 0.01 × 10 4 . This is  comparable  with  previously  reported  helium  permeances  of  81 × 10 7 mol/(m 2 ⋅ s ⋅ Pa) [20] for MFI membranes with a thickness of around 500  nm.  The  helium  permeance  reduces  drastically  as n -hexane  is introduced  due  to  blocking  of  helium  transport  through  the  zeolite pores. This drastic change is characteristic of a high quality membrane with few defects [21]. The helium permeance decreased by 99.7 % at an n -hexane relative pressure of 0.023, corresponding to a defect width of 0.94  nm. As  the  relative  pressure  was  further  increased  to  0.80,  the helium permeance reduced to 0.05 × 10 7 mol/(m 2 ⋅ s ⋅ Pa), i.e. decreased by 99.9 %. This relative pressure corresponds to a defect width of 17 nm. Table 1 also shows that the relative area (defect area/membrane area) of these defects with a size larger than 17 nm is estimated to be as low as 0.01 × 10 4 . However, the relative area of micropore defects ( < 2.2 nm),

texts.orig: Here, J is the flux (mol/(m 2 ⋅ s)), θ o and θ L are the adsorbed loadings on the feed side and permeate side respectively, which were calculated using IAST. Csat is the adsorbed concentration at saturation, and ε is the fraction of pores of the membrane area. Consequently, the high single gas CH4 flux at 300 K is a result of relatively high surface permeability ( α * determined to 3.1 × 10 3 m/s in previous work [29]) in combination with a relatively high loading and difference in loading over the membrane θ 0 θ L = 0.05, i.e. driving force [29]. The relatively high loading of CH4 is a result of high heat of adsorption. The heats of adsorption of CH4,  N2 and  He  in  MFI  are 20, 16  and 4.8  kJ/mol  [30,31], respectively. The somewhat lower flux of N2 at 300 K is a result of lower loading (lower heat of adsorption) and driving force ( θ 0 θ L = 0.01), although the surface permeability ( α * = 6.5 × 10 3 m/s [29]) is slightly larger. The much lower flux of He at 300 K is a result of very low loading and driving force ( θ 0 θ L = 2.7 × 10 4 ), although the surface permeability is high ( α * = 9.5 × 10 2 m/s [29]).

texts.text: 3.3. Separation of an equimolar CH4/N2/he mixture

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Fig.  3  shows  the  separation  factor  and  flux  of  the  membrane  for separation of an equimolar CH4/N2/He mixture at a feed pressure of 3 bar and a permeate pressure of 0.2 bar from room temperature to 120 K.
texts.text: Fig.  3  shows  the  separation  factor  and  flux  of  the  membrane  for separation of an equimolar CH4/N2/He mixture at a feed pressure of 3 bar and a permeate pressure of 0.2 bar from room temperature to 120 K.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The membrane was highly selective towards CH4 and N2 over He in the studied temperature range. A maximum separation factor of CH4/He and  N2/He  of  265  and  50  were  observed  at  an  optimum  separation temperature  of  150  and  170  K,  respectively.  The  high  selectivity  is mainly a result of high adsorption selectivity of CH4 and N2 over He due to the much higher heat of adsorption of CH4 and N2 compared to He. Fig. 3 also shows the adsorption selectivities of CH4/He and N2/He as a function of temperature at 3 bar. The adsorption selectivities increased with  decreasing  temperature,  which  explained  why  the  membrane selectivity increased with decreasing temperature at temperatures above the optimum temperature. The decreasing membrane selectivity with decreasing temperature at temperatures lower than the optimum temperature could be ascribed to decreased driving force for CH4 and N2 as discussed above for the decreasing flux in the single gas case. However, the driving force for He was still increasing, which therefore resulted in a lower membrane selectivity at lower temperature. At 150 K, the CH4 flux was as high as 1.1 mol/(m 2 ⋅ s) although the partial pressure difference of CH4 across the membrane was as low as 0.8 bar. The high and almost constant CH4 flux as a function of temperature observed for the mixture was comparable to that observed for the single component case.

texts.orig: Table  S1  summarizes  the  permeance,  permeate  composition  and separation selectivity for the separation of the mixture at a feed pressure of 3 bar and a permeate pressure of 0.2 bar. A maximum CH4 permeance of 135 × 10 7 mol/(m 2 ⋅ s ⋅ Pa) was observed at 167 K. At this temperature, the CH4/He separation selectivity was 270 and the N2/He separation selectivity was 50. In this case, the He permeance was only 0.5 × 10 7 mol/(m 2 ⋅ s ⋅ Pa) and the He concentration was only 0.36% in the permeate stream. The CH4 permeance decreased slightly to 133 × 10 7 mol/(m 2 ⋅ s ⋅ Pa) when the temperature decreased to 153 K. Meanwhile, the  He  permeance  decreased  to  0.42 × 10 7 mol/(m 2 ⋅ s ⋅ Pa),  which resulted in a very high CH4/He separation selectivity of 315. In this case, the He concentration was 0.33% in the permeate stream. Therefore, the membrane can efficiently remove CH4 and N2 from He with very low loss of He at these conditions.

texts.orig: A MFI membrane process for recovery of helium from natural gas, see Fig. 6 was designed using an incremental membrane model with 123 increments in total for the first stage. In each increment of membrane area, the permeances of CH4, N2 and He were assumed constant as 133, 16  and  0.42 × 10 7 mol/(m 2 ⋅ s ⋅ Pa),  respectively,  i.e.  the  same  as measured experimentally for an equimolar mixture as shown in Fig. 5. The feed composition at each increment was estimated from a material balance. The feed was assumed to be a helium-rich natural gas with a composition of CH4/N2/He 90%/7%/3% [3] and a flowrate of 3 mol/s (240 m 3 /h). In this case, the composition of the gas varies and is not the same as the equimolar ternary mixture used in the present work or the equimolar binary mixtures used in previous work [6]. The first membrane stage in Fig. 6 was assumed to operate at 153 K and a feed pressure of 3 bar and a permeate pressure of 0.1 bar. The retentate from the first stage with a composition of CH4/N2/He 3.8%/47%/49% was fed to the second stage for removal of N2. Stage 1 is mainly separating CH4 from the  gas  and  Table  2  shows  the  estimated  adsorption  selectivities  of CH4/He for the mixture used in the experiments as well as for the feed to, and retentate from, the first stage. The estimated adsorption selectivity for  CH4/He  for  the  equimolar  mixture  used  in  the  experiment  is in-between  the  estimated  adsorption  selectivities  for  the  feed  and retentate mixtures. Stage 2 is mostly separating N2 from the gas, and in this case, an analogous situation was observed, for the estimated N2/He adsorption  selectivities.  Since  the  selectivity  of  the  membrane  is dependent  on  the  adsorption  selectivity,  the  assumed  permeances should  be  a  good  approximation  of  the  average  permeances  for  the process.  In  addition,  in  reality,  the  permeances  and  the  selectivities would probably vary with the composition of the gas, but it is beyond the scope of the present work to determine the membrane performance as a function of concentration. Consequently, the process illustrated in Fig. 6 is only an approximation of a real process.

texts.orig: The second stage (732 increments) in Fig. 6 was assumed to operate at 124 K and a feed pressure of 5 bar and a permeate pressure of 0.05 bar, which are the optimum separation conditions for N2/He mixture as reported  in  our  previous  work  [6].  A  N2 permeace  of  39 × 10 7 mol/(m 2 ⋅ s ⋅ Pa) and a He permeance of 0.51 × 10 7 mol/(m 2 ⋅ s ⋅ Pa) were assumed  [6].  In  addition,  a  methane  permeance  of  50 × 10 7 mol/(m 2 ⋅ s ⋅ Pa) was assumed, see Table S2. The permeate from the second stage was assumed to be recycled. Fig. 6 shows that for upgrading of 3 mol/s CH4/N2/He (90%/7%/3%) natural gas, only ca 1.6 m 2  membrane area is needed to reach a purity of about 99% He. As the membranes are quite selective, the helium loss was as low as 3.2% in this simple process.
texts.text: The second stage (732 increments) in Fig. 6 was assumed to operate at 124 K and a feed pressure of 5 bar and a permeate pressure of 0.05 bar, which are the optimum separation conditions for N2/He mixture as reported  in  our  previous  work  [6].  A  N2 permeace  of  39 × 10 7 mol/(m 2 ⋅ s ⋅ Pa) and a He permeance of 0.51 × 10 7 mol/(m 2 ⋅ s ⋅ Pa) were assumed  [6].  In  addition,  a  methane  permeance  of  50 × 10 7 mol/(m 2 ⋅ s ⋅ Pa) was assumed, see Table S2. The permeate from the second stage was assumed to be recycled. Fig. 6 shows that for upgrading of 3 mol/s CH4/N2/He (90%/7%/3%) natural gas, only ca 1.6 m 2  membrane area is needed to reach a purity of about 99% He. As the membranes are quite selective, the helium loss was as low as 3.2% in this simple process.

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: 4. Conclusions
texts.text: 4. Conclusions

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Ultra-thin  MFI  membranes  were  evaluated  for  permeation  of  the single components and for separation of a CH4/N2/He mixture at low temperatures and different feed pressures. The membranes displayed high separation factor and flux at all studied conditions. The high separation performance resulted from the high adsorption selectivities of CH4/He and N2/He. Higher fluxes were observed for CH4  and N2  at higher feed pressure whereas the separation factor was slightly lower due to the lower adsorption selectivity. The high flux and permeance was attributed to the ultra-thin zeolite films and open supports. Consequently, a simple membrane process can be designed to recover helium from natural gas efficiently. These results indicate that ultra-thin MFI membranes are promising candidates for this application.
